project_overview.project_development_objective: To facilitate a sustainable expansion of Pakistan's electricity generation capacity and strengthen WAPDA's capacity to develop the country's hydropower resources by installing a 1,410 MW hydropower plant on an existing dam with an already constructed tunnel
project_overview.total_financing: US$914 million (IBRD Loan: US$400 million, IDA Credit: US$440 million equivalent, WAPDA: US$74 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries: millions of energy users including industry, households, and farmers. Indirect beneficiaries: non-electric users through higher productivity and employment, particularly in industrial sector

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Partial - Pakistan faces significant security challenges and conflict in border areas; the project area itself is relatively secure but located in a country with governance and security concerns

key_components.component_name: Component A: Construction of Power House and Modification to the Tunnel
key_components.description: Civil works including construction of power house, penstock connecting Tunnel 4 to power units, and raised intakes for Tunnels 3 and 4

key_components.component_name: Component B: Power Units and Ancillary Equipment
key_components.description: Installation of three 470 MW turbines and generators, transformers, electro-mechanical equipment, and short transmission line to grid connection

key_components.component_name: Component C: Social Action and Environmental Management Plans, Dam Monitoring and Surveillance
key_components.description: Implementation of Social Action Plan (US$15M), Environmental Management Plan (US$3M), Dam Safety Monitoring (US$5M), and Glacier Monitoring Program (US$6M)

key_components.component_name: Component D: Construction Supervision, Monitoring and Evaluation
key_components.description: Construction supervision consulting services (US$24M) and M&E of project impacts and management plans (US$2.4M)

key_components.component_name: Component E: Project Management Support, Capacity Building, Technical Assistance and Training
key_components.description: PMU support and audits (US$14M), WAPDA capacity building and IPOE (US$4M), and future project preparation (US$16M)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Water and Power Development Authority (WAPDA), specifically through the Project Management Unit (PMU) established under the General Manager Tarbela's office
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: PMU headed by Project Director with three units: Engineering Unit (Civil, Mechanical, Electrical), Procurement/Financial Management/Administration Unit, and Environment & Social Unit. Supported by Construction Supervision Consultants (CSCs), Monitoring and Evaluation Consultants (M&ECs), and Independent Panel of Experts (IPOE)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: International Competitive Bidding (ICB) for major contracts; National Competitive Bidding (NCB) for smaller contracts; shopping procedures for minor works; direct contracting for specialized services. Two major ICB contracts: civil works (US$317.8M) and turbines/generators (US$431.9M)
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Quarterly progress reports (45 days after quarter end), annual reports (by September 30), mid-term review (October 31, 2016), and Implementation Completion Report (6 months after closing). M&ECs provide independent monitoring of project impacts, EMP, and SAP implementation

identified_risks.risk_description: Efficiency and transparency in procurement and financial management could be challenging given the large size of the project. Limited capacity of internal audit function at WAPDA
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Proper procurement planning and tracking system; experienced procurement consultants; internationally recruited CSCs as engineer; IPOE involvement; Bank team placement in-country; strengthening of internal audit function; website for procurement transparency

identified_risks.risk_description: Risks of inadequate technical designs and engineering works leading to failure or poor performance. Large scale of operation represents a risk
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Internationally renowned design company; IPOE involvement throughout project; world-leading turbine/generator manufacturer; WAPDA's previous experience with Tunnel 3 conversion; state-of-the-art design concepts and methods

identified_risks.risk_description: Outstanding resettlement issues from Tarbela Dam Project (40 claims) and Ghazi Barotha Project (410 claims) pending in courts. Poor implementation of original Tarbela resettlement program left difficult legacy issues. Risk of opposition from affected communities and NGOs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Social Action Plan (SAP) developed to address legacy issues; Resettlement Claims Commission established; social mobilization team contracted; clear boundaries to cover only in-court cases; Grievance Redress Committee on-site; extensive stakeholder consultations conducted

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental impacts primarily limited to construction stage but include potential for landslides, slope instability, dust, noise, water quality impacts, and disturbance to fauna. Located in seismically active region
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Environmental and Social Assessment (ESA) and Environmental Management Plan (EMP); Environmental and Social Management Unit (ESMU) within PMU; independent M&ECs for supervision; Environmental Code of Practices in contracts; monitoring programs for water quality, air quality, noise, slope stability; tree plantation plan; dam safety monitoring upgrades

identified_risks.risk_description: Pakistan faces significant security challenges and conflict in border areas. Project is considered potential terrorist target. Persistence of conflict affects all aspects of life in Pakistan
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project area has restricted access and security arrangements; Independent Panel of Experts; regular Bank supervision; contingency planning; emergency preparedness plan

identified_risks.risk_description: Governance is a considerable concern in Pakistan. Perception of corruption has increased. Large contracts present rent-seeking opportunities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Governance and Accountability Action Plan (GAAP); website disclosure of all procurement information; public bid openings with CSO presence; conflict of interest declarations; multiple oversight entities (M&ECs, IPOE, internal audit); complaints mechanism; Bank sanctions framework; enhanced transparency measures

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential impact of climate change on inflow hydrology; construction cost overruns; construction delays; variations in water availability
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Extensive hydrological simulations using 1962-2009 data; sensitivity analysis showing robustness to 70% decline in generation; probabilistic risk assessment; scenario analysis; monitoring of glaciers and climate impacts; contingency planning

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of sediment blockage of tunnel intakes; sedimentation of reservoir reducing useful life; potential for dam safety issues
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Raised intake construction to prolong operational life; dam safety monitoring system upgrades; sediment management studies; glacier monitoring program; regular dam inspections; IPOE review of dam safety aspects

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Category A (Full Assessment required due to large-scale construction on major dam with potential impacts on densely populated downstream areas)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Grievance Redress Committee (GRC) established on-site with representatives from WAPDA, contractors, local communities, social organizers, and civil society. Public Information Centre with public register for grievances. GRC meets regularly to review grievances with decisions delivered within 10 days. Complaints mechanism also established for procurement-related issues with website tracking
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender consultations carried out during ESA preparation at 10 different locations with 96 female stakeholders. Women-specific concerns addressed in Social Impact Management Framework. Women's participation encouraged in construction employment through female working groups. Women's mobility and safety addressed in Traffic Management Plan. Women's health and education facilities identified as community needs in social assistance program
vulnerable_groups: Previously resettled persons from Tarbela Dam Project (96,000 people resettled in 1968-1976)
Previously resettled persons from Ghazi Barotha Project (179 families/899 persons)
Outstanding claimants in courts (40 from TDP, 410 from GBHP)
Low-income households in project area (majority below poverty line)
Women and children (safety concerns during construction)
Local communities in Haripur and Swabi districts with limited access to services

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project addresses conflict sensitivity through: (1) Social Action Plan to resolve outstanding resettlement disputes from previous projects that could fuel grievances; (2) Extensive stakeholder consultations to build consensus and address concerns; (3) Grievance Redress Committee and multiple accountability mechanisms to prevent conflicts; (4) Communications strategy to manage expectations and provide transparent information; (5) Employment opportunities for local communities to build support; (6) Governance and Accountability Action Plan to prevent corruption-related conflicts; (7) Security arrangements appropriate to Pakistan's context
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Pakistan faces significant fragility factors: (1) Persistence of conflict in border areas affecting all aspects of life; (2) Weak governance with corruption concerns (Transparency International ranking 143 in 2010); (3) Security challenges throughout the country; (4) Economic challenges with GDP growth of 2.4% in FY10/11, inflation at 13.7%; (5) Fiscal deficit of 6.3% of GDP; (6) Circular debt in power sector estimated at PKR 270-310 billion; (7) High unemployment and poverty (17.2% in 2007-08); (8) Weak institutional capacity and governance in public sector; (9) Limited access to basic services in project area (water, sanitation, health, education)
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project area has restricted access and security arrangements. Project is considered potential terrorist target. Pakistan faces significant security challenges with conflict in border areas. Mitigation measures include: (1) Restricted access to WAPDA-controlled area; (2) Security staff training; (3) Emergency preparedness planning; (4) Regular Bank supervision; (5) Independent Panel of Experts oversight; (6) Contingency planning for security incidents; (7) Awareness raising on security risks for workers and communities
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: No new displacement expected from T4HP itself as project uses existing dam and tunnel on WAPDA-owned land. However, project addresses legacy displacement issues: (1) Tarbela Dam Project (1968-1976) displaced 96,000 people and acquired 33,200 hectares; (2) Ghazi Barotha Project (1995-2003) displaced 179 families (899 persons); (3) Outstanding resettlement claims: 40 from TDP, 410 from GBHP pending in courts; (4) Social Action Plan developed to settle these legacy claims through out-of-court commission with estimated compensation of PKR 1,909 million; (5) Resettlement Claims Commission established to facilitate faster resolution; (6) Social mobilization team contracted to support settlement process
